title: Eat — and soften it with หน่อย
pedagogy: Vocabulary: กิน (mid ก + live น → mid), หน่อย (ห-nam น: high + mai ek → low). Pattern: หน่อย after the thing shrinks a request into a favor (ขอน้ำหน่อย); กินข้าว 'eat rice' = have a meal, closing the loop with กินข้าวหรือยัง from unit 1.

exercises.title: หน่อย makes any request smaller
body: หน่อย literally means “a little” — but tucked after a request it's pure politeness: ขอน้ำหน่อย ≈ “could I just get some water?”.
It isn't about quantity. You'll get a full glass; the หน่อย just shrinks the imposition.
And now กินข้าวหรือยัง from Greetings decodes itself: eat-rice-yet? — กิน + ข้าว were hiding in there all along.

exercises.meaning: Could I get some water, please? (female speaker)
exercises.gloss: ask-for + water + softener + female polite particle
exercises.explanation: ขอ …หน่อย ครับ/ค่ะ — the full polite request frame. Everything else you ever order slots into the middle.

exercises.meaning: To eat / have a meal (lit. “eat rice”).
exercises.explanation: กินข้าว is “to eat”, full stop — rice stands in for every meal, noodle dinners included.

exercises.context: You want more water at a restaurant. You're a man. Flag the waiter and ask nicely.
exercises.question: What do you say?

choices.thai: ขอน้ำหน่อยครับ

exercises.explanation: ขอ + หน่อย + ครับ is the full courtesy frame. เอาน้ำ is what you say to a friend holding the bottle — and bare น้ำ is just naming a liquid at a stranger.
